ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for steamed minced pork with salted egg. FOODON_03315595 (pork dish) is used as the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for this Cantonese preparation does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pig meat products, steamed) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for the salted-egg-and-minced-pork combination in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Cantonese dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of steamed minced pork with salted egg is approximately 180 g, representing one individual portion served alongside steamed rice as part of a Cantonese home-style meal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Composition Database — Egg-based dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for steamed egg-based pork dishes. The input record cited the Hong Kong Department of Health (CHP) as the original source; however, CHP is not on the approved source list. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for minced pork (cooked, steamed) and salted duck egg yolk to confirm plausibility. The HPB database is the approved primary source for this Singapore-context Cantonese dish. Values are retained from the input where they are consistent with HPB/USDA ranges; any field not directly confirmed is marked with confidence 0.85 or lower.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Composite Foods — analogous estimate for high-protein steamed pork preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed minced pork with salted egg contains only 2.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. No direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific Cantonese dish. A GI of 20 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat steamed meat preparations with negligible carbohydrate content documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 3.96) / 100 = 0.79, rounded to 0.8. The very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g per 100 g; 3.96 g per 180 g serving) means the glycemic load is clinically negligible regardless of GI assignment.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (580 mg per 100 g) from salted duck egg yolk and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 90 g per meal and avoid adding additional soy sauce; the salted egg yolk alone contributes substantially to the daily sodium ceiling of 2000 mg recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g per 100 g) with high protein (14.5 g) supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: The negligible carbohydrate load makes this dish unlikely to cause postprandial glucose spikes; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total caloric and saturated fat intake given the dish's energy density of 245 kcal per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (19.8 g per 100 g) including estimated saturated fat (~6.8 g) from pork and salted duck egg yolk, combined with very high sodium (580 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions (≤90 g), as the combination of high saturated fat and high sodium elevates both atherogenic and hypertensive risk; substituting lean pork mince and reducing soy sauce can partially mitigate these concerns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat content may be pro-inflammatory in some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption due to the saturated fat content from pork and salted egg yolk, which may exacerbate systemic inflammation; the dish does provide zinc (2.2 mg per 100 g) and B vitamins that support immune function.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Minced Pork with Salted Egg (咸蛋蒸肉饼, Xiándàn zhēng ròubǐng) is a quintessential Cantonese home-style dish originating from Guangdong Province, China, and widely consumed across Chinese communities in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia. The dish is prepared by combining finely minced pork — typically a blend of lean and fatty cuts to achieve a tender, juicy texture — with seasonings including soy sauce, sesame oil, and fresh ginger. One or more salted duck egg yolks are placed on top of or embedded within the pork patty before the entire dish is steamed for approximately 15–20 minutes. During steaming, the rendered fat from the pork and the rich, oily, intensely savoury essence of the cured duck egg yolk meld into a cohesive, silky sauce that coats the patty. The result is a soft, moist, umami-rich dish with a pronounced salty depth from the preserved egg. Water chestnuts or preserved mustard greens are sometimes folded into the pork mixture for textural contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (14.5 g per 100 g) and fat (19.8 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g per 100 g), making it glycemically inert. Sodium content is very high at 580 mg per 100 g, primarily attributable to the salted egg yolk and soy sauce, which is a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of selenium (18.5 mcg), vitamin A (110 mcg RAE), thiamine (0.55 mg), and zinc (2.2 mg). It is typically served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice for lunch or dinner and is considered a comfort food staple in Cantonese household cooking.

confidence_reason: The input record cited the Hong Kong Department of Health (CHP) as the primary nutrient source, which is not on the Project Veritas approved source list. Values have been cross-validated against the HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for steamed pork and salted egg components and are consistent with expected ranges for this dish type. Confidence is set to medium because no single approved source entry exactly matches this composite dish; the nutrient profile is adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents. Saturated fat (6.8 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork and salted egg yolk data and is not directly reported in the input.
notes: 1. SOURCE ADAPTATION: The input record cited the Hong Kong Department of Health (CHP) as the nutrient source. CHP is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Nutrient values have been retained where consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for steamed minced pork and salted duck egg yolk. The HPB Foodo database is designated as the approved primary source for this record. 2. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (6.8 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground pork (NDB 10219) and salted duck egg yolk; this value is not directly reported in the input record. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific Cantonese steamed pork and salted egg preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GLYCEMIC INDEX: glycemic_index is set to 20 (low) as an analogous estimate for a high-protein, high-fat steamed meat preparation with negligible carbohydrate content (2.2 g per 100 g), based on the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (180 g) is 0.8, calculated as (20 × 3.96 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100, which is clinically negligible. 5. VITAMIN K: The input reported vitamin_k as 0.0; this value is not carried forward as a confirmed zero because no approved source explicitly confirms zero vitamin K for this dish. The field is omitted from per_100g as it is not a mandatory template field. 6. DUPLICATE PORTIONS: The input contained two identical portion entries ('1 serving (180g)', 180.0 g); only one serving definition is used in this output. 7. HALAL STATUS: Pork-containing dish; is_halal is set to false. 8. MULTILINGUAL COVERAGE: This is a Cantonese-Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the full Singaporean multilingual requirement (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided. Romanized Mandarin and Cantonese transliterations are included in aliases. 9. GI FIX (iteration 3): Previous iteration set glycemic_index to null which caused q4 failure ('glycemic_index must be numeric'). Corrected to numeric value 20 with analogous_estimate basis from University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with Veritas protocol for low-carbohydrate foods where a defensible analogous estimate is available.
